prediction.prompt: A traffic jam can crawl backwards down a motorway while every car in it moves forwards. Where does the jam “live”?
options: In one blocked car at the front
In no single car — only in the pattern of the whole
prediction.reveal: The jam is an emergent pattern of the arrangement, not a property of any car. This is the book’s first lesson: the many can have properties none of the one possesses.

questions.prompt: “The whole is more than the sum of its parts,” taken precisely, means a collection can:
options: Have properties absent from every individual part
Never be predicted by any science
questions.explanation: Emergence names properties that belong to the arrangement and cannot be found by studying a part in isolation — wetness, a colony’s intelligence, the phantom jam.

questions.prompt: A single ant is nearly witless, yet the colony farms, wages war, and repairs itself. The colony’s intelligence is best described as:
options: An emergent property of many local interactions
questions.explanation: No ant holds the plan; the capacity appears at the level of the many, from local interaction plus nonlinearity.

questions.prompt: If emergence is real, studying one neuron ever harder will:
options: Eventually reveal the mind in full
Never by itself reveal decisions that belong to the whole brain
Prove the mind does not exist
questions.explanation: Weak emergence: the higher-level behaviour is derivable only by letting the parts interact (often only by simulation), not by inspecting a part alone.

prediction.prompt: Order can appear in a system with no leader, blueprint, or external hand arranging it. Most likely?
options: Possible — local rules plus energy can self-organise
prediction.reveal: Self-organisation: global order arising from local interaction without central control — from convection cells to sand ripples to markets.

questions.prompt: Self-organisation differs from mere emergence by emphasising:
options: Order arising spontaneously from local rules without central control
questions.explanation: Self-organisation is the mechanism; the resulting pattern is the emergent property.

questions.prompt: Bénard convection cells form neat hexagons when a fluid is heated from below. This shows order can be paid for by:
options: A throughput of energy dissipated by the system
questions.explanation: Self-organised order is typically sustained by energy flowing through an open system, not created from nothing.

questions.prompt: Remove the energy input from a self-organised pattern and it will usually:
questions.explanation: Dissipative structures depend on continued throughput; cut it and the pattern relaxes.

prediction.prompt: A flock wheels as one. How many rules does each simulated bird need to reproduce it?
prediction.reveal: Reynolds’ boids need only three local rules — separation, alignment, cohesion — with no leader and no global plan. Try it in the simulator below.

questions.prompt: The three boids rules are separation, alignment and:
questions.explanation: Each bird steers to avoid crowding (separation), match neighbours’ heading (alignment), and move toward the local centre (cohesion).

questions.prompt: In the boids model there is no lead bird. The flock’s coordinated turn is therefore:
options: Emergent from local rules applied by every bird
questions.explanation: Global coordination emerges from identical local rules — the essence of decentralised order.

questions.prompt: Raise separation far above cohesion in the simulator. The flock will most likely:
questions.explanation: Cohesion pulls together; separation pushes apart. Tip the balance and the flock disperses.

prediction.prompt: Termites build vast, ventilated mounds with no architect and no shared plan. They coordinate mainly through:
options: A blueprint held by the queen
Signals left in the shared environment
prediction.reveal: Stigmergy: individuals coordinate by modifying a shared environment that in turn cues the next action — the work itself is the memory.

questions.prompt: Stigmergy means coordination through:
options: Traces left in a shared environment
questions.explanation: An ant’s pheromone trail or a termite’s deposited pellet changes the environment, cueing others — indirect coordination.

questions.prompt: Ants converge on the shortest route to food because pheromone on shorter paths:
options: Is chosen by a leader ant
Accumulates faster and evaporates less per trip
questions.explanation: Shorter round-trips reinforce their trail more per unit time, so the shortest path wins by positive feedback.

questions.prompt: Wikipedia edits and open-source commits resemble stigmergy because contributors respond to:
options: The current state of the shared artefact
questions.explanation: The evolving document/codebase is the shared environment that cues the next contribution.

prediction.prompt: A microphone near its own speaker produces a screech that grows until it saturates. This runaway is an example of:
prediction.reveal: Positive feedback amplifies; negative feedback stabilises. Collective behaviour is largely a story of which loop dominates.

questions.prompt: Negative feedback tends to:
options: Damp deviations toward a set point
questions.explanation: A thermostat is negative feedback: it counteracts change and stabilises. Positive feedback compounds change.

questions.prompt: A bank run spreads as withdrawals trigger more withdrawals. The dominant loop is:
questions.explanation: Each withdrawal raises the incentive to withdraw — self-amplifying, the signature of a cascade.

questions.prompt: A collective system with only positive feedback and no damping will most likely:
options: Run to an extreme or collapse
questions.explanation: Unchecked amplification drives systems to saturation or breakdown; stability needs some negative feedback.

prediction.prompt: As you slowly heat water, its temperature rises smoothly — then at 100°C something abrupt happens. Collective systems often behave:
options: With sudden phase-transition-like jumps at thresholds
prediction.reveal: Phase transitions: small continuous changes in a control parameter can trigger a sudden, qualitative shift once a threshold is crossed.

questions.prompt: A phase transition is marked by:
options: A sudden qualitative change at a critical point
questions.explanation: Near the critical point, tiny changes in the control parameter produce a large, abrupt shift in the system’s state.

questions.prompt: A protest stays small for weeks, then explodes in a day. Framed as a phase transition, the crowd crossed:
questions.explanation: Once enough people join to tip others’ thresholds, participation cascades — a social critical point.

questions.prompt: Near a critical point, systems typically show:
questions.explanation: Critical systems become highly sensitive, with fluctuations spanning many scales — a warning sign of imminent transition.

prediction.prompt: Everyone in a crowd has a personal “tipping point” — how many others must act before they will. A riot can ignite even if:
options: Almost no one individually wants a riot
prediction.reveal: Granovetter’s threshold model: the outcome depends on the whole distribution of thresholds, not on average attitude. Explore it in the simulator.

questions.prompt: In Granovetter’s model, a person’s threshold is:
options: Their private opinion of the act
The number/fraction of others who must act first before they join
questions.explanation: Behaviour depends on how many others have already acted, not just on private preference.

questions.prompt: Two crowds have identical average attitudes but different threshold distributions. They can:
options: Produce wildly different outcomes — one calm, one rioting
questions.explanation: One missing ‘threshold-1’ instigator can be the difference between nothing and a full cascade.

questions.prompt: In the simulator, removing the lowest-threshold person often:
options: Can stop the entire cascade from starting
questions.explanation: Cascades need a spark low enough to go first; remove it and the chain never begins.

prediction.prompt: A “complex adaptive system” (an economy, an immune system, an ecosystem) is best steered by:
options: Shaping incentives and letting agents adapt
prediction.reveal: Complex adaptive systems are made of many adapting agents; they respond to rules and incentives, often defeating precise central control.

questions.prompt: A complex adaptive system consists of many agents that:
options: Adapt their behaviour based on experience and interaction
questions.explanation: Adaptation and interaction produce emergent, often surprising, system-level behaviour.

questions.prompt: A regulator bans one risky product; firms invent a similar one. This illustrates that adaptive agents:
questions.explanation: Because agents adapt, interventions reshape rather than simply stop behaviour — a hallmark of CAS.

questions.prompt: Compared with a simple machine, a complex adaptive system is:
options: Harder to control precisely, better steered by incentives
questions.explanation: You nudge distributions and incentives; you rarely command exact outcomes.

prediction.prompt: Why do prey animals bunch together when a predator appears? Hamilton’s answer:
options: Each individual selfishly shrinks its own danger zone by hiding behind others
prediction.reveal: The “selfish herd”: aggregation can arise from each individual reducing its own risk, not from group altruism. Cohesion needn’t be cooperative.

questions.prompt: Hamilton’s selfish herd explains grouping as the result of each animal:
questions.explanation: If each moves to put others between itself and danger, a tight aggregation emerges from pure self-interest.

questions.prompt: A tight fish school forms with no coordination signal. The selfish-herd reading says it is:
options: An emergent by-product of individual risk-reduction
questions.explanation: Group structure can emerge without any group-level intention.

questions.prompt: The selfish herd warns us that visible cooperation-looking behaviour:
options: May be explained by individual self-interest
questions.explanation: Apparent togetherness is not proof of altruism — a recurring caution in the book.

prediction.prompt: In a herd of 100, your personal chance of being the one taken by a single predator, versus being alone, is:
options: Lower — risk is diluted across many
prediction.reveal: The dilution effect: for a given attack, being one of many lowers each individual’s probability of being the victim.

questions.prompt: The dilution effect reduces individual risk because:
options: A single attack is spread over more potential victims
questions.explanation: Per-capita risk from one predator event falls as group size rises — ‘safety in numbers’, arithmetic version.

questions.prompt: Dilution can favour grouping even if the group attracts more predators, provided:
questions.explanation: What matters to each individual is per-capita risk, not total attention drawn.

questions.prompt: Dilution is a benefit that requires:
options: No cooperation at all — just numbers
questions.explanation: It is a statistical benefit of being one-of-many, needing no cooperation.

prediction.prompt: A dense, swirling school of fish makes a predator’s job harder mainly by:
options: Overloading its ability to lock onto a single target
prediction.reveal: The confusion effect: many similar moving targets degrade a predator’s ability to single one out and attack successfully.

questions.prompt: The predator confusion effect works by:
options: Impairing target selection among many similar moving prey
questions.explanation: Sensory and attentional limits make it hard to track one target in a shifting many.

questions.prompt: Confusion is strongest when prey in a group are:
questions.explanation: Oddity breaks confusion; uniformity strengthens it — hence the cost of standing out.

questions.prompt: An ‘odd’ differently-coloured fish in a school is likely to be:
questions.explanation: Predators exploit oddity to defeat confusion, pressuring prey toward uniformity.

prediction.prompt: A larger group can spot an approaching predator sooner because:
options: More eyes scan more of the surroundings
prediction.reveal: The many-eyes effect: collective vigilance rises with group size, so each individual can afford to look up less and feed more.

questions.prompt: The many-eyes effect lets each individual in a big group:
options: Spend less time scanning while detection stays high
questions.explanation: Detection is a collective good; individuals trade personal vigilance for feeding as numbers grow.

questions.prompt: Collective vigilance is vulnerable to free-riding because each animal is tempted to:
options: Scan less and rely on others
questions.explanation: If everyone under-scans hoping others watch, detection can quietly erode — a public-goods problem.

questions.prompt: As group size increases, individual scanning typically:
options: Falls while group-level detection stays adequate
questions.explanation: This is the empirical signature of the many-eyes trade-off.

prediction.prompt: Group living clearly has benefits. Does it also carry real costs?
options: Yes — competition, disease, conspicuousness and more
prediction.reveal: Grouping is a balance sheet: dilution and many-eyes on one side; competition for food, disease transmission, and visibility to predators on the other.

questions.prompt: A major cost of group living is:
questions.explanation: Proximity spreads pathogens; density also intensifies competition for food and mates.

questions.prompt: Optimal group size is set where:
options: Groups are as large as possible
Marginal benefit of joining equals marginal cost
Groups are as small as possible
questions.explanation: Animals should join until the next member’s cost outweighs its benefit — a marginal balance.

questions.prompt: Because costs and benefits both scale with size, real groups tend to be:
questions.explanation: Trade-offs produce intermediate optima rather than runaway aggregation.

prediction.prompt: A vampire bat shares blood with an unrelated roost-mate that fed it before. This is best explained by:
options: Reciprocal altruism — help now, be helped later
prediction.reveal: Reciprocal altruism: cooperation between non-kin can be stable when individuals interact repeatedly and remember who reciprocated.

questions.prompt: Reciprocal altruism is stabilised mainly by:
options: Repeated interaction with memory of past behaviour
questions.explanation: Repeated games plus the ability to detect and punish cheats make ‘help now, helped later’ pay.

questions.prompt: Reciprocity breaks down when interactions become:
questions.explanation: Without a shadow of the future, defection dominates — the classic prisoner’s-dilemma result.

questions.prompt: For reciprocal altruism, a good cognitive prerequisite is:
questions.explanation: You must remember who cooperated to reward them and withhold from cheats.

prediction.prompt: A ground squirrel gives an alarm call that endangers itself but warns relatives. Hamilton’s rule says this spreads when:
prediction.reveal: Kin selection / inclusive fitness: helping relatives can propagate the helper’s genes. Hamilton’s rule: rb > c.

questions.prompt: Hamilton’s rule rb > c says altruism spreads when:
options: Relatedness times benefit to recipient exceeds cost to actor
questions.explanation: Genes for helping kin can increase in frequency via the reproduction of relatives — inclusive fitness.

questions.prompt: A worker bee forgoing reproduction to raise the queen’s offspring is explained by:
options: High relatedness making indirect fitness pay
questions.explanation: Haplodiploidy and high relatedness make helping kin a route to genetic success.

questions.prompt: As relatedness r falls toward zero, kin-selected altruism should:
questions.explanation: With low r, the rb term shrinks and altruism no longer clears Hamilton’s threshold.

prediction.prompt: A peacock’s enormous tail is a survival handicap. Why would such waste be honest signalling?
options: Only high-quality individuals can afford the cost, so it can’t be faked cheaply
prediction.reveal: The handicap principle: signals are believable when they are costly enough that low-quality fakers can’t afford them.

questions.prompt: Costly signalling keeps signals honest because cost:
options: Is higher for low-quality fakers, deterring bluffs
questions.explanation: Differential cost — cheaper for the genuinely fit — makes the signal a reliable index of quality.

questions.prompt: A gazelle ‘stotting’ (leaping high) in front of a predator likely signals:
options: ‘I’m fit; chasing me is futile’ — an honest, costly display
questions.explanation: Only fit gazelles can afford to waste energy stotting, so predators believe it.

questions.prompt: Make a signal cheap to produce and it will tend to become:
questions.explanation: Cheap signals invite fakery; costliness is what underwrites honesty.

prediction.prompt: At a country fair, 787 people guessed an ox’s weight. Galton found the crowd’s median guess versus the true weight was:
options: Almost exactly right — within a pound
prediction.reveal: Galton’s ox: aggregated independent guesses can beat almost every individual. Try it in the estimator below.

questions.prompt: Crowd estimates beat most individuals because independent errors:
questions.explanation: Over- and under-estimates offset, leaving the shared signal — provided guesses are independent.

questions.prompt: In the simulator, letting participants copy each other before guessing tends to:
questions.explanation: Copying destroys independence, so errors no longer cancel — the central caution of Book One.

questions.prompt: The crowd’s advantage is largest when guesses are:
questions.explanation: Independence plus diversity maximises error cancellation.

prediction.prompt: Each juror is right 60% of the time and votes independently. As the jury grows, the probability the majority is correct:
prediction.reveal: Condorcet’s Jury Theorem: if each member is better than chance and votes independently, majority accuracy climbs toward certainty with size. Explore the reversal below.

questions.prompt: Condorcet’s theorem requires each voter to be:
questions.explanation: Competence above 0.5 plus independence drives majority accuracy up with N.

questions.prompt: If each voter is only 40% accurate (below chance), enlarging the group makes the majority:
questions.explanation: The theorem runs in reverse below 0.5 — numbers amplify systematic error.

questions.prompt: Correlated voters (all reading the same source) break the theorem because they:
options: Effectively count as far fewer independent voters
questions.explanation: Shared error means the effective N is small — independence is the load-bearing assumption.

prediction.prompt: A crowd’s error depends on its members’ average error and their diversity. Adding a member who is individually worse but thinks differently can:
options: Improve the crowd by adding diversity
prediction.reveal: The diversity prediction theorem: Crowd error = Average individual error − Diversity. Diversity is not a nicety; it is subtracted straight off the error.

questions.prompt: The diversity prediction theorem states crowd error equals:
questions.explanation: Greater spread of independent predictions mathematically lowers the collective error.

questions.prompt: A team of near-identical experts will, by this theorem, have:
options: Low diversity, so little crowd bonus
questions.explanation: Similarity means small diversity term, so the crowd barely beats the average member.

questions.prompt: The theorem implies that, for a fixed average ability, you should prefer members who are:
questions.explanation: Diversity is subtracted from error, so difference is directly valuable.

prediction.prompt: Surowiecki lists conditions for a wise crowd. Which single ingredient, if lost, most reliably turns wisdom into folly?
prediction.reveal: Surowiecki’s four conditions: diversity, independence, decentralisation, and aggregation. Losing independence is the classic route to madness of crowds.

questions.prompt: Surowiecki’s four conditions are diversity, independence, decentralisation and:
questions.explanation: You need a way to combine dispersed private judgments into one estimate.

questions.prompt: An online poll where everyone sees the running tally before voting most violates:
questions.explanation: Seeing others’ answers first correlates judgments and can trigger herding.

questions.prompt: Decentralisation matters because it lets the crowd draw on:
questions.explanation: Dispersed local knowledge is exactly what aggregation can harvest — if independence is preserved.

prediction.prompt: Averaging many independent estimates shrinks error because the noise part of each estimate is:
prediction.reveal: Independent-error cancellation is the engine under the wisdom of crowds: shared signal survives averaging; independent noise washes out.

questions.prompt: Averaging helps most when individual errors are:
questions.explanation: Independent errors cancel; correlated errors survive averaging and can even dominate.

questions.prompt: If a rumour biases everyone’s guess in the same direction, averaging will:
questions.explanation: Common bias is correlated error — averaging cannot cancel what everyone shares.

questions.prompt: Doubling the number of independent estimates reduces the noise’s standard error by roughly:
questions.explanation: Standard error of the mean falls as 1/√N — diminishing but real returns to numbers.

prediction.prompt: The Delphi method asks experts to answer in private rounds, then share anonymous summaries and revise. The anonymity is there to:
options: Protect independence from status and bandwagons
prediction.reveal: Delphi engineers the wise-crowd conditions on purpose: private judgment, anonymity to prevent dominance, structured feedback, iteration toward convergence.

questions.prompt: Delphi uses anonymity chiefly to prevent:
options: Dominance by loud or senior voices
questions.explanation: Anonymous rounds protect independence so status doesn’t stampede the group.

questions.prompt: Compared with an open committee meeting, Delphi is designed to reduce:
questions.explanation: Structured, anonymous iteration curbs the very forces that wreck open discussion.

questions.prompt: Delphi is essentially an attempt to:
questions.explanation: It manufactures independence, diversity and aggregation deliberately.

prediction.prompt: Prediction markets let people bet on outcomes. Their forecasts are often accurate because prices:
options: Aggregate dispersed information and reward being right
prediction.reveal: Prediction markets aggregate private information via prices and give a financial incentive for accuracy over posturing.

questions.prompt: A prediction market’s price is best read as:
options: A probability-like aggregate of participants’ information
questions.explanation: Trading pulls dispersed knowledge into a single price that behaves like a probability.

questions.prompt: Prediction markets can fail when they are thin and dominated by a few correlated traders because:
questions.explanation: Small, correlated crowds lose the error-cancellation that makes markets wise.

questions.prompt: A key advantage of markets over polls is that they:
options: Attach a cost to being wrong, filtering cheap talk
questions.explanation: Skin in the game weights informed views and penalises bluffing.

prediction.prompt: Machine-learning ‘random forests’ average many mediocre decision trees and often beat one carefully tuned model because:
options: Averaging diverse weak learners cancels their independent errors
prediction.reveal: Ensemble methods are the wisdom of crowds in code: combine many diverse, decorrelated weak models and their errors cancel.

questions.prompt: Ensemble methods improve accuracy by combining models that are:
questions.explanation: Bagging and feature randomness decorrelate the trees so their errors offset — exactly the crowd principle.

questions.prompt: If every tree in a forest is trained identically on the same data, the ensemble gains:
questions.explanation: Without diversity there is nothing to cancel — the same failure as a room of identical experts.

questions.prompt: Random forests deliberately inject randomness in order to:
questions.explanation: Decorrelation is the whole point — diversity engineered on purpose.

prediction.prompt: Research finds a group’s ‘collective intelligence’ (factor c) predicts performance across tasks. It correlates most with:
prediction.reveal: Collective intelligence ‘c’: a group-level ability that depends more on equal participation and social perceptiveness than on members’ average or peak IQ.

questions.prompt: The collective intelligence factor c is predicted best by:
options: Equality of conversational turn-taking and social sensitivity
questions.explanation: Groups where participation is balanced and members read each other well score higher across varied tasks.

questions.prompt: A team dominated by one talker tends to have:
questions.explanation: Domination suppresses the independent, diverse input that raises collective intelligence.

questions.prompt: To raise a team’s c, the most reliable lever is to:
questions.explanation: Process — how the group interacts — outweighs raw individual horsepower.
